UX / UI Case Study — Healthcare

Rebuilding trust into a multi-specialty
medical homepage.

Complete Medical Wellness treats spine, orthopedic, and pain patients across ten-plus locations in New Jersey and New York — but its homepage buried the one thing that mattered most: getting a worried patient to a booked appointment.

View the live site — completemedicalwellness.com ↗
Role
UX/UI Designer
Scope
Homepage Redesign
Deliverables
Desktop & Mobile UI
Tools
Figma

Healthcare decisions are trust decisions before they're anything else. A patient landing on this homepage is often in pain, unsure which specialist they need, and deciding — in seconds — whether this practice feels credible enough to call.

Complete Medical Wellness is a real multi-specialty group offering orthopedics, spine care, pain management, physical therapy, chiropractic care, and vascular care, with same-day telemedicine and coverage across more than ten locations. The existing homepage had all of that substance, but its structure worked against the person trying to find it — long uneven text blocks, repeated sections, and calls-to-action with no context for what they'd lead to.

// Side by side

The old homepage, next to the redesign

Same practice, same information — restructured around what a patient actually needs first. Scroll inside each frame to compare the full page.

BEFORE Live site, current homepage
AFTER Redesign concept, 2026
// Audit

Reading the existing homepage like a chart

Before redesigning anything, I audited the live site against what a first-time patient actually needs on arrival: a clear next step, visible credibility, and a fast path to the right specialist.

User journeyPath to booking
Unclear
Information architectureContent structure
Poor
Service discoveryFinding the right specialty
Weak
Trust signalsAbove the fold
Absent
Visual hierarchyScanability
Flat
Conversion pathsCTAs & booking access
Limited
Diagnosis: the content and credentials were strong — the interface simply wasn't organized around a patient's decision, so trust and urgency never reached the surface.
// Problems, in detail

Four issues doing the most damage

01

No guided journey

Patients weren't steered toward booking, finding a specialist, selecting a treatment, or locating a clinic. Multiple "Contact Us" buttons appeared with no context for what happened next.

02

Buried key actions

Location info was mixed into marketing copy, telemedicine sat too far down the page, and the sections that mattered most were pushed well below the fold.

03

Cognitive overload

Understanding which specialties and treatments applied to a visitor's condition required extensive scrolling through repetitive, similarly-weighted content.

04

No trust above the fold

Doctor credentials, patient reviews, awards, insurance acceptance, and patient volume — the exact signals healthcare decisions run on — were missing from the first screen.

// Strategy

A conversion-driven approach, built for trust

The redesign strategy centered on three goals: guide every visitor toward a clear next step, surface credibility immediately, and present the practice as a premium, coordinated multi-specialty provider rather than a loose collection of services.

Trust signals promoted to the first screen

Patient volume, specialist count, locations, Google rating, and insurance acceptance now appear in a stat bar immediately below the hero — before a visitor has to scroll for reassurance.

A booking widget embedded in the hero

Instead of a generic "Contact Us," a consultation form sits directly in the first viewport, with a preferred-location field so multi-clinic visitors don't have to hunt for their nearest office.

Service discovery simplified into a grid

Six specialties are presented as scannable cards with icons and one-line descriptions, replacing dense paragraphs, so a visitor can identify their condition in seconds.

A premium multi-specialty identity

Specialist profiles with credentials and photos, verified patient testimonials, and a dedicated telemedicine section reposition the practice as an integrated, board-certified network — not a directory of services.

// The redesign

Desktop & mobile, built from the strategy

Full-length homepage screens, from the hero through footer. Scroll inside each frame to view the whole page.

↕ scroll to view full desktop page
HERO

Split hero with embedded booking form

Headline, specialty framing, and trust badges sit left; a live "Book a Free Consultation" form sits right — reachable without a single scroll.

STAT BAR

Credibility, immediately

Patients treated, specialist count, locations, rating, and insurance acceptance form a dark stat strip directly under the fold.

SPECIALTIES

Six services, one glance

Icon-led cards replace paragraphs of copy, each with a direct "Learn More" path into the relevant service.

TEAM

Named, credentialed specialists

Photos, credentials, and years of experience per doctor, each with a direct "Book" action — turning trust into a booking in one tap.

TELEMEDICINE

Virtual care given real estate

A dedicated dark-themed section now explains same-day virtual visits with a visible average wait time, instead of being mentioned in passing.

LOCATIONS

Every clinic, mapped and actionable

Ten-plus locations are laid out with direct "Directions" and "Book Here" actions per clinic, rather than a single generic list.

↕ scroll for full mobile page
// Before / after

What changed, and why it matters

AspectBeforeAfter
First action Multiple undifferentiated "Contact Us" buttons Hero booking form with service & location fields
Trust signals No credentials, reviews, or stats above the fold Patient count, ratings, insurance acceptance in a stat bar under the hero
Services Long text blocks, scroll-heavy discovery Six-card icon grid, scannable in seconds
Specialists Not featured on the homepage Named profiles with credentials and per-doctor booking
Telemedicine Mentioned low on the page, low visual weight Dedicated section with wait time and clear CTA
Locations Location details mixed into marketing copy Structured cards per clinic with directions & booking
Visual hierarchy Uniform weight, weak typographic scale Clear scale, consistent CTA styling, deliberate spacing
// Intended impact

What this structure is designed to move

This redesign is a concept project rather than a shipped, measured one — so these are the outcomes the structure is built to drive, not reported analytics.

1st screen
Booking access, no scroll required
6 cards
Replacing paragraph-based service discovery
5 signals
Trust metrics surfaced above the fold
10+
Locations made individually actionable

Next step to validate this design: ship it against the live homepage and A/B test booking-form completion rate and scroll depth to first CTA.

// Reflection

What I'd carry into the next healthcare project

Trust is a layout decision, not just a copy decision

The original site had every credibility signal a patient needs — it just lived too far down the page. Moving trust content above the fold was a bigger lever than any new content would have been.

Multi-specialty needs multi-path navigation

A single generic CTA can't serve someone choosing between six specialties. Giving each service, doctor, and location its own direct action point reduced the decision entirely to "which one is mine."
